schools.summary: The renewed demand before the plague of frogs: 'and the LORD spake unto Moses, Go unto Pharaoh, and say unto him, Thus saith the LORD, Let my people go, that they may serve me' — God sending Moses again to Pharaoh with the demand 'Let my people go' (Exodus 5:1; 7:16, the renewed summons, before the second plague, the frogs, the persistence of God's word to the hardened king, the demand pressed); 'Let my people go' — the demand of the whole contest (repeated before each plague, God claiming Israel as his own, the release pressed despite Pharaoh's refusal, Exodus 7:13-14); 'that they may serve me' (the worship of God the end of the deliverance, redeemed to serve, Exodus 4:23, the demand pressed before the plague, the providence, the plague to follow the refusal, vv.2-6) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: And the LORD spake unto Moses, Go unto Pharaoh, and say unto him, Thus saith the LORD, Let my people go, that they may serve me
the renewed demand before the plague of frogs
the demand of the whole contest, pressed before each plague (Exodus 5:1; 7:16)
the worship of God the end of the deliverance (Exodus 4:23)

schools.summary: The threat of the plague of frogs: 'and if thou refuse to let them go, behold, I will smite all thy borders with frogs' — God threatening, if Pharaoh refused, to smite all Egypt with frogs (the second plague announced, the frogs swarming from the Nile, vv.3-6, the warning before the judgment, the plague conditioned on the refusal); the condition (the plague following the refusal) — the warning (the judgment hanging on Pharaoh's obstinacy); 'I will smite all thy borders with frogs' (the frogs, from the Nile, the river Egypt worshipped, now turned to a plague, the swarming creatures filling the land, vv.3-4, the judgment on Egypt, perhaps on the frog-deity, the Egyptians' false gods judged, Exodus 12:12, the plague announced) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke.

themes: And if thou refuse to let them go, behold, I will smite all thy borders with frogs
the threat of the plague of frogs
the second plague announced, conditioned on the refusal (vv.3-6)
the judgment on Egypt and its false gods (Exodus 12:12)

schools.summary: The frogs invading every place: 'and the river shall bring forth frogs abundantly, which shall go up and come into thine house, and into thy bedchamber, and upon thy bed, and into the house of thy servants, and upon thy people, and into thine ovens, and into thy kneadingtroughs' — the frogs swarming up from the Nile in abundance and invading every place: houses, bedchambers, beds, ovens, kneading-troughs (the all-pervading plague, no place free from the loathsome creatures, the judgment penetrating everywhere); the frogs in the most private and intimate places (the bedchamber, the bed) — the inescapable plague (the loathsome creatures everywhere, the judgment that reached even the king's chamber); 'into thine ovens, and into thy kneadingtroughs' (the frogs in the very places of food, ovens, kneading-troughs, the defilement of all, the judgment that spoiled even the bread, the all-pervading affliction) — Gill, Wesley, Clarke.

themes: And the river shall bring forth frogs abundantly, which shall go up and come into thine house, and into thy bedchamber, and upon thy bed... and into thine ovens, and into thy kneadingtroughs
the frogs invading every place
the all-pervading, inescapable plague reaching even the king's chamber
the loathsome judgment defiling even the bread

schools.summary: The frogs upon Pharaoh and all his people: 'and the frogs shall come up both on thee, and upon thy people, and upon all thy servants' — the frogs coming up upon Pharaoh himself, his people, and his servants (the plague upon all, none exempt, from the king to the meanest, the loathsome creatures crawling upon the persons of all, the judgment that spared none of the Egyptians); the frogs upon all (Pharaoh, people, servants) — the universal plague (the judgment reaching every Egyptian, the loathsome affliction on all persons); 'upon all thy servants' (the frogs upon the whole court and people, the all-pervading judgment, the loathsome creatures upon every person, the contrast with Israel, spared in Goshen, vv.22, the plague on all Egypt) — Gill.

themes: And the frogs shall come up both on thee, and upon thy people, and upon all thy servants
the frogs upon Pharaoh and all his people
the universal plague sparing none, from the king to the least
the loathsome judgment on all Egypt (contrast Israel spared, vv.22)

schools.summary: The command to bring up the frogs: 'and the LORD spake unto Moses, Say unto Aaron, Stretch forth thine hand with thy rod over the streams, over the rivers, and over the ponds, and cause frogs to come up upon the land of Egypt' — God commanding Aaron to stretch his rod over the waters (streams, rivers, ponds) and bring up the frogs (the second plague executed, the frogs from all the waters, the rod the instrument, as the first plague, Exodus 7:19, the judgment brought); the rod stretched over the waters (the instrument of the plague, Exodus 7:19) — the sign of God's power (the bringing up of the frogs); 'cause frogs to come up upon the land of Egypt' (the frogs brought up from all the waters, the second plague executed, the judgment on Egypt, the Nile, its god, breeding the plague, the loathsome creatures covering the land, v.6) — Calvin, Gill, Clarke.

themes: And the LORD spake unto Moses, Say unto Aaron, Stretch forth thine hand with thy rod over the streams... and cause frogs to come up upon the land of Egypt
the command to bring up the frogs
the rod the instrument of the plague (Exodus 7:19)
the second plague brought from all the waters of Egypt

schools.summary: The frogs covering the land: 'and Aaron stretched out his hand over the waters of Egypt; and the frogs came up, and covered the land of Egypt' — Aaron stretching out his rod, and the frogs coming up from the waters and covering all the land (the second plague executed, the frogs swarming everywhere, vv.3-4, the judgment fulfilled, the loathsome creatures covering Egypt); the frogs coming up (from all the waters, in vast numbers) — the swarming plague (the loathsome creatures emerging); 'covered the land of Egypt' (the frogs covering all Egypt, the all-pervading plague, vv.3-4, the judgment fulfilled, the land overrun, the providence, the plague that would bring Pharaoh's first relenting, v.8) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And Aaron stretched out his hand over the waters of Egypt; and the frogs came up, and covered the land of Egypt
the frogs covering the land
the second plague executed, the loathsome judgment overrunning all Egypt (vv.3-4)
the plague that would bring Pharaoh's first relenting (v.8)

schools.summary: The magicians imitating the plague of frogs: 'and the magicians did so with their enchantments, and brought up frogs upon the land of Egypt' — the Egyptian magicians (as with the rod-serpent and the blood, Exodus 7:11, 22) imitating the plague, bringing up frogs by their enchantments (the counterfeit, Satan's imitation of God's works, permitted to harden Pharaoh, the magicians able to add to the plague, but not to remove it, v.8, the limit of their power soon reached, v.18); the magicians' imitation (by enchantments, the deceiving arts, perhaps with demonic aid or sleight) — the counterfeit of God's miracle (the hardening of Pharaoh, encouraged by the imitation); 'brought up frogs upon the land of Egypt' (the magicians could add frogs, imitate the plague, but could not remove them, Pharaoh forced to beg Moses, v.8, the limit of the counterfeit, the magicians could do harm, not good, add to the plague, not relieve it, the providence, the imitation hardening Pharaoh, until the limit at the lice, v.18-19) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And the magicians did so with their enchantments, and brought up frogs upon the land of Egypt
the magicians imitating the plague of frogs
the counterfeit of God's work, permitted to harden Pharaoh (Exodus 7:11)
able to add to the plague but not remove it (v.8), the limit soon reached (vv.18-19)

schools.summary: Pharaoh's first relenting under the plague: 'then Pharaoh called for Moses and Aaron, and said, Intreat the LORD, that he may take away the frogs from me, and from my people; and I will let the people go, that they may do sacrifice unto the LORD' — Pharaoh, overwhelmed by the frogs (his magicians able to add but not remove them, v.7), calling for Moses and begging him to entreat the LORD to take away the plague (the first crack in the proud king, the judgment forcing him to appeal to the LORD he had defied, Exodus 5:2, the relenting under pressure); Pharaoh's appeal (begging Moses to pray to the LORD) — the proud king, who said 'Who is the LORD?' (Exodus 5:2), now forced to seek the LORD's mercy (the judgment bringing the defiant to beg, the appeal of necessity, not of repentance); 'I will let the people go, that they may do sacrifice unto the LORD' (Pharaoh's promise, the first, made under the pressure of the plague, the seeming yielding, which he would break when the plague was removed, v.15, the false repentance, the relenting of necessity, not of the heart, the providence, the plague forcing the promise, soon broken) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: Then Pharaoh called for Moses and Aaron, and said, Intreat the LORD, that he may take away the frogs... and I will let the people go, that they may do sacrifice unto the LORD
Pharaoh's first relenting under the plague
the proud king who defied the LORD (Exodus 5:2) now begging his mercy
the promise of necessity, not repentance, soon broken (v.15)

schools.summary: Moses giving Pharaoh the choice of the time: 'and Moses said unto Pharaoh, Glory over me: when shall I intreat for thee, and for thy servants, and for thy people, to destroy the frogs from thee and thy houses, that they may remain in the river only?' — Moses giving Pharaoh the honor of naming the time when the frogs should be removed (the offer, that Pharaoh might choose the hour, to prove the deliverance was of God, not chance, the confidence of Moses, sure God would answer at the appointed time); 'Glory over me' (have this advantage, appoint the time) — Moses letting Pharaoh set the time, that the removal at the exact hour might prove it the LORD's doing (not natural, the demonstration of God's power, the sign that it was no chance); 'that they may remain in the river only' (the frogs to be removed from the land and people, left only in the river, their natural place, the complete removal, the deliverance to prove the LORD's hand, vv.10-13) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: And Moses said unto Pharaoh, Glory over me: when shall I intreat for thee... to destroy the frogs... that they may remain in the river only?
Moses giving Pharaoh the choice of the time
the offer that Pharaoh appoint the hour, to prove it the LORD's doing
the demonstration of God's power, no chance (vv.10-13)

schools.summary: The purpose of the sign - that there is none like the LORD: 'and he said, To morrow. And he said, Be it according to thy word: that thou mayest know that there is none like unto the LORD our God' — Pharaoh choosing the next day (tomorrow) for the removal of the frogs (perhaps hoping they might depart naturally, or simply delaying), Moses agreeing ('be it according to thy word') — the removal to come at the appointed time (the proof set); the great purpose — 'that thou mayest know that there is none like unto the LORD our God': the removal at the exact appointed time was to prove to Pharaoh that there is none like the LORD (the incomparable God, the true and living God, above all the gods of Egypt, Exodus 15:11, 'Who is like unto thee, O LORD, among the gods?', the purpose of the plagues, that Egypt and Pharaoh might know the LORD, Exodus 7:5; 8:22; 9:14, 16, the answer to Pharaoh's 'Who is the LORD?', Exodus 5:2 — that there is none like him, the demonstration of the LORD's incomparable power and sovereignty); 'that thou mayest know that there is none like unto the LORD our God' (the plagues teaching the knowledge of God, that the LORD is incomparable, the only true God, the purpose of the judgments, the glory of God, the knowledge of his name, the demonstration of the incomparable God) — JFB, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

debates.question: What was the purpose of removing the frogs at the appointed time?

sides.position: To prove there is none like the LORD - the incomparable God above all Egypt's gods (Exodus 15:11), the purpose of the plagues (that Egypt might know the LORD, Exodus 7:5; 9:16), the answer to Pharaoh's 'Who is the LORD?' (Exodus 5:2)

themes: And he said, To morrow. And he said, Be it according to thy word: that thou mayest know that there is none like unto the LORD our God
the purpose of the sign - that there is none like the LORD
the incomparable God demonstrated above all Egypt's gods (Exodus 15:11)
the purpose of the plagues, the answer to 'Who is the LORD?' (Exodus 7:5; 5:2)

schools.summary: The promised removal of the frogs: 'and the frogs shall depart from thee, and from thy houses, and from thy servants, and from thy people; they shall remain in the river only' — Moses declaring the frogs would depart from Pharaoh, his houses, servants, and people, remaining only in the river (their natural place, the complete removal promised, the deliverance to come at the appointed time, v.10, the proof of God's hand); the frogs departing from all (the complete removal) — the deliverance promised (the plague to be lifted); 'they shall remain in the river only' (the frogs left only in the river, their natural habitat, the complete removal from the land and people, the deliverance, proving the LORD's power, the removal at the exact time, v.13) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the frogs shall depart from thee, and from thy houses, and from thy servants, and from thy people; they shall remain in the river only
the promised removal of the frogs
the complete removal at the appointed time (v.10)
proving the LORD's hand (v.13)

schools.summary: Moses crying to the LORD to remove the frogs: 'and Moses and Aaron went out from Pharaoh: and Moses cried unto the LORD because of the frogs which he had brought against Pharaoh' — Moses, having promised the removal (vv.10-11), going out and crying (praying earnestly) to the LORD to take away the frogs he had brought (as the instrument, the prayer of the intercessor, the earnest cry, that God would fulfill the promise and prove his hand); Moses' earnest prayer (crying to the LORD) — the intercession (the dependence on God, Moses, though the instrument, praying for the removal, not presuming); 'because of the frogs which he had brought against Pharaoh' (Moses praying for the removal of the plague he, by God, had brought, the intercessor seeking the deliverance, the providence, God answering the prayer at the appointed time, v.13) — Gill.

themes: And Moses and Aaron went out from Pharaoh: and Moses cried unto the LORD because of the frogs which he had brought against Pharaoh
Moses crying to the LORD to remove the frogs
the intercessor's earnest prayer (vv.10-11)
the dependence on God, praying for the removal at the appointed time (v.13)

schools.summary: The LORD answering and the frogs dying: 'and the LORD did according to the word of Moses; and the frogs died out of the houses, out of the villages, and out of the fields' — God answering Moses' prayer (v.12) at the appointed time (v.10, tomorrow), and the frogs dying throughout the houses, villages, and fields (the deliverance fulfilled, the removal at the exact time, proving God's hand, the answer to prayer); God answering Moses' prayer (the removal at the appointed time) — the proof of God's hand (the exact fulfillment, the answer to the intercessor); 'the frogs died out of the houses, out of the villages, and out of the fields' (the frogs dying everywhere, the complete removal, the deliverance from the plague, the proof, the removal at the exact hour, v.10, none like the LORD) — Gill.

themes: And the LORD did according to the word of Moses; and the frogs died out of the houses, out of the villages, and out of the fields
the LORD answering and the frogs dying
the deliverance at the appointed time, the answer to prayer (v.10)
the removal at the exact hour, proving none like the LORD

schools.summary: The frogs gathered in stinking heaps: 'and they gathered them together upon heaps: and the land stank' — the dead frogs gathered into great heaps, and the land stinking with the stench of their decay (the aftermath of the plague, the loathsome stench, the reminder of the judgment, even the removal leaving a stench, the lingering effect of the plague); the frogs gathered in heaps (the vast multitude, even dead) — the magnitude of the plague (the heaps of the dead creatures); 'the land stank' (the stench of the rotting frogs, the loathsome aftermath, the reminder that even the lifting of judgment left its mark, the providence, the stench a witness to the plague, yet Pharaoh would still harden, v.15) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And they gathered them together upon heaps: and the land stank
the frogs gathered in stinking heaps
the loathsome aftermath, the stench a witness to the plague
yet Pharaoh would still harden (v.15)

schools.summary: Pharaoh hardening when the respite came: 'but when Pharaoh saw that there was respite, he hardened his heart, and hearkened not unto them; as the LORD had said' — Pharaoh, finding relief (Hebrew, a breathing, a relief, the frogs gone, a breathing-space from the plague), hardening his heart and breaking his promise (v.8, the false repentance exposed, the relenting under the plague vanishing when the pressure lifted, the self-hardening of the king); Pharaoh 'hardened his heart' — here, notably, Pharaoh hardening his own heart (the active self-hardening, as in the earlier plagues, Exodus 7:13, 22; 8:32, the hardening that precedes the 'LORD hardened' language of the later plagues, Exodus 9:12, the willful obstinacy, the king, relieved, returning to his defiance, the lesson on false repentance — the relenting that is only of necessity, under pressure, vanishing the moment the pressure is removed, the heart unchanged, the promise broken when the fear is gone, the warning against the repentance of the afflicted that is not of the heart, Psalm 78:34-37, 'when he slew them, then they sought him... nevertheless they did flatter him with their mouth'; Hosea 6:4, the danger of the respite hardening rather than softening, mercy abused to further obstinacy, Romans 2:4-5); 'as the LORD had said' (Pharaoh's hardening exactly as God had foretold, Exodus 4:21; 7:4, the foreknown obstinacy, the providence, the self-hardening overruled by God to multiply the signs, Exodus 7:3-4, and show his power, Exodus 9:16; Romans 9:17-18, the foretold hardening) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

debates.question: What does Pharaoh's hardening when the respite came teach?

sides.position: The lesson on false repentance - the relenting under the plague exposed as merely of necessity, vanishing the moment the pressure lifted (the heart unchanged, Psalm 78:34-37; Hosea 6:4); the self-hardening of the king (Exodus 7:13; 8:32), the respite abused to further obstinacy (mercy abused, Romans 2:4-5), the obstinacy foreknown and overruled by God (Exodus 4:21; Romans 9:17-18)

themes: But when Pharaoh saw that there was respite, he hardened his heart, and hearkened not unto them; as the LORD had said
Pharaoh hardening when the respite came (the crux of the false repentance)
the relenting of necessity vanishing when the pressure lifted, the heart unchanged (Psalm 78:34-37; Hosea 6:4)
the self-hardening (Exodus 7:13; 8:32), the respite abused (Romans 2:4-5), foreknown and overruled by God (Exodus 4:21)

schools.summary: The command for the plague of lice: 'and the LORD said unto Moses, Say unto Aaron, Stretch out thy rod, and smite the dust of the land, that it may become lice throughout all the land of Egypt' — God commanding Aaron to smite the dust, that it might become lice (gnats, or lice, small biting insects) throughout Egypt (the third plague, without warning, unlike the first two, the dust itself turned to the plague, the judgment from the very ground); the dust smitten (the very dust of Egypt turned to lice) — the judgment from the lowliest element (the power of God, turning dust to living creatures, beyond the magicians' power, v.18); 'that it may become lice throughout all the land of Egypt' (the lice, gnats, swarming everywhere from the dust, the third plague, the loathsome, tormenting insects, the judgment on Egypt, the dust of the land they boasted now their torment, the plague that the magicians could not imitate, v.18) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: And the LORD said unto Moses, Say unto Aaron, Stretch out thy rod, and smite the dust of the land, that it may become lice throughout all the land of Egypt
the command for the plague of lice
the third plague, without warning, the very dust turned to the tormenting insects
the judgment beyond the magicians' power (v.18)

schools.summary: The dust becoming lice on man and beast: 'and they did so; for Aaron stretched out his hand with his rod, and smote the dust of the earth, and it became lice in man, and in beast; all the dust of the land became lice throughout all the land of Egypt' — Aaron smiting the dust, and it becoming lice upon man and beast throughout Egypt (the third plague executed, the lice tormenting all, men and animals, the dust turned to the swarming insects, the judgment fulfilled); the lice on man and beast (the biting, tormenting insects upon all living) — the loathsome affliction (the judgment on the persons of the Egyptians and their cattle); 'all the dust of the land became lice throughout all the land of Egypt' (the whole dust of Egypt turned to lice, the vast, all-pervading plague, the judgment from the very ground, the power of God, beyond the magicians, v.18) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And they did so; for Aaron stretched out his hand with his rod, and smote the dust of the earth, and it became lice in man, and in beast; all the dust of the land became lice throughout all the land of Egypt
the dust becoming lice on man and beast
the third plague, the very ground turned to the tormenting insects
the all-pervading judgment, beyond the magicians' power (v.18)

schools.summary: The magicians' failure at the lice: 'and the magicians did so with their enchantments to bring forth lice, but they could not: so there were lice upon man, and upon beast' — the Egyptian magicians, who had imitated the rod-serpent, the blood, and the frogs (Exodus 7:11, 22; 8:7), trying to bring forth lice by their enchantments, but could not (the limit of their power reached, the counterfeit failing, the first plague they could not imitate); the magicians' failure ('they could not') — the limit of Satan's imitation (the counterfeit reaching its bound, the magicians, permitted thus far to harden Pharaoh, now stopped, the lesson — the powers of darkness can imitate God only so far, and only by God's permission, and are at last confounded, the limit of the counterfeit, the impotence of the false against the true, the providence, God setting the bound of the imitation, that his power might be seen the greater, and the magicians forced to confess, v.19); 'so there were lice upon man, and upon beast' (the lice remaining, the plague the magicians could not remove or even imitate, the judgment standing, the limit of the counterfeit exposed, the magicians confounded, v.19) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And the magicians did so with their enchantments to bring forth lice, but they could not: so there were lice upon man, and upon beast
the magicians' failure at the lice
the limit of Satan's imitation reached, the counterfeit confounded (Exodus 7:11)
the powers of darkness imitate God only so far, and by his permission, God's power seen the greater

schools.summary: The magicians' confession 'the finger of God' and Pharaoh's hardening: 'then the magicians said unto Pharaoh, This is the finger of God: and Pharaoh's heart was hardened, and he hearkened not unto them; as the LORD had said' — the magicians' words — 'This is the finger of God': the magicians, unable to imitate the plague (v.18), confessing to Pharaoh that this was 'the finger of God' — the work of a divine power, beyond their art and beyond nature (the forced confession of the enemies of God, the very magicians owning the LORD's hand, the 'finger of God,' the immediate power of God, an easy work of his finger, cf. Psalm 8:3; Luke 11:20, 'If I with the finger of God cast out devils,' the testimony wrung from the opponents, the counterfeiters confessing the true power they could not match, the witnesses noting both that the confession was true, it was indeed God's finger, and that it was the confession of necessity, the magicians owning the power, yet not converted, as Pharaoh hardened still); 'the finger of God' (the easy, immediate working of God's power, what to God is but a finger's work confounds all the wisdom and power of Egypt, the divine hand owned by the enemies, the limit of the creature against the Creator); 'Pharaoh's heart was hardened, and he hearkened not unto them; as the LORD had said' (Pharaoh, even with his own magicians confessing the finger of God, still hardening his heart, the obstinacy resisting even the testimony of his own counsellors, the hardening exactly as God foretold, Exodus 7:3-4, the providence, the obstinacy overruled to multiply the signs, Romans 9:17-18, the heart that would not yield to evidence) — Calvin, Wesley, Henry, Gill.

debates.question: What does the magicians' 'This is the finger of God' signify?

sides.position: The forced confession of the enemies of God - the magicians owning the divine power they could not match (the immediate working of God, Luke 11:20), the testimony wrung from the opponents; true, yet of necessity, not conversion; and Pharaoh hardening even against his own magicians' witness (Exodus 7:3-4; Romans 9:17-18)

themes: Then the magicians said unto Pharaoh, This is the finger of God: and Pharaoh's heart was hardened, and he hearkened not unto them; as the LORD had said
the magicians' confession 'the finger of God' and Pharaoh's hardening
the forced confession of the enemies owning the divine power they could not match (Luke 11:20)
Pharaoh hardening even against his own magicians' witness, foreknown and overruled (Exodus 7:3-4; Romans 9:17-18)

schools.summary: The renewed demand before the plague of flies: 'and the LORD said unto Moses, Rise up early in the morning, and stand before Pharaoh; lo, he cometh forth to the water; and say unto him, Thus saith the LORD, Let my people go, that they may serve me' — God sending Moses to meet Pharaoh early, as he came forth to the water (the Nile, perhaps for worship of the river, or his morning custom, the meeting at the water, the place of the first plague, Exodus 7:15, the renewed demand before the fourth plague, the flies, the persistence of God's word); the renewed demand ('Let my people go,' Exodus 5:1; 7:16; 8:1) — the keynote pressed again before the plague (God claiming Israel, the release demanded); 'that they may serve me' (the worship of God the end, Exodus 4:23, the demand before the plague, the providence, the plague of flies to follow the refusal, vv.21-24) — Keil and Delitzsch, Henry, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And the LORD said unto Moses, Rise up early in the morning, and stand before Pharaoh; lo, he cometh forth to the water... Let my people go, that they may serve me
the renewed demand before the plague of flies
the meeting at the water (Exodus 7:15), the keynote pressed again (Exodus 8:1)
the worship of God the end (Exodus 4:23)

schools.summary: The threat of the plague of flies: 'else, if thou wilt not let my people go, behold, I will send swarms of flies upon thee, and upon thy servants, and upon thy people, and into thy houses: and the houses of the Egyptians shall be full of swarms of flies, and also the ground whereon they are' — God threatening, if Pharaoh refused, to send swarms of flies (or beetles, gadflies, a mixture of noxious insects) upon Pharaoh, his people, and houses (the fourth plague announced, the swarms filling the land, the warning before the judgment); the 'swarms' (the Hebrew arob, a mixture, perhaps of various biting, noxious insects, or the dog-fly, or beetle) — the tormenting swarms (the loathsome, biting plague, the judgment filling the houses and ground); 'the houses of the Egyptians shall be full of swarms of flies, and also the ground whereon they are' (the swarms filling the houses and covering the ground, the all-pervading plague, the judgment on Egypt, with, now, the distinction of Goshen, v.22) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: Else, if thou wilt not let my people go, behold, I will send swarms of flies upon thee... and the houses of the Egyptians shall be full of swarms of flies
the threat of the plague of flies
the fourth plague announced (the swarms of noxious insects)
the all-pervading judgment, with the distinction of Goshen to come (v.22)

schools.summary: God severing Goshen from the plague: 'and I will sever in that day the land of Goshen, in which my people dwell, that no swarms of flies shall be there; to the end thou mayest know that I am the LORD in the midst of the earth' — the great word — 'I will sever (set apart, distinguish) in that day the land of Goshen... that no swarms of flies shall be there': from this fourth plague onward, God making a marked distinction between Egypt and Goshen, where Israel dwelt — the flies would fill Egypt but not come into Goshen (the severance, God putting a difference between his people and the Egyptians, the world, the distinction that displayed God's special care of his own, Israel exempt from the plague, the witnesses noting the lesson — God distinguishes between his people and the world, Exodus 9:4, 26; 11:7; Malachi 3:18, 'then shall ye... discern between the righteous and the wicked,' sheltering his own in the day of judgment, a type of the safety of God's people in the great judgment, the sealing, Revelation 7:3, the providence guarding his own); the purpose — 'that thou mayest know that I am the LORD in the midst of the earth': the distinction to prove that the LORD is in the midst of the earth (present, ruling, distinguishing, not a distant or local god, but the LORD governing all, sparing whom he will, the knowledge of God's particular providence, the LORD ruling in the earth, distinguishing his own, the answer to Pharaoh, the LORD present and sovereign); the severing of Goshen — God's special care of his people (a token of the covenant, the distinction of grace, the providence, the people sheltered, the world judged, the LORD in the midst of the earth, distinguishing his own) — Calvin, Wesley, Gill, Ellicott.

debates.question: What does God's severing of Goshen display?

sides.position: God putting a difference between his people and the world (Exodus 9:4; 11:7; Malachi 3:18), sheltering his own in the day of judgment (a type of the sealing, Revelation 7:3); 'that thou mayest know I am the LORD in the midst of the earth' - the LORD present and sovereign, ruling and distinguishing, the particular providence guarding his own

themes: And I will sever in that day the land of Goshen, in which my people dwell, that no swarms of flies shall be there; to the end thou mayest know that I am the LORD in the midst of the earth
God severing Goshen from the plague (the crux of the distinction)
God putting a difference between his people and the world, sheltering his own (Exodus 9:4; 11:7; Malachi 3:18; Revelation 7:3)
'the LORD in the midst of the earth' - present and sovereign, the particular providence guarding his own

schools.summary: The division set between Israel and Egypt: 'and I will put a division between my people and thy people: to morrow shall this sign be' — God declaring he would put a division (a redemption, a setting apart, the Hebrew suggesting a ransom or deliverance) between his people (Israel) and Pharaoh's people (Egypt, the distinction set, Israel spared, Egypt plagued, v.22, the sign to come on the morrow, the appointed time, as the frogs, v.10); the 'division' (the marked difference, God's people set apart from the world) — the distinction of grace (the sheltering of God's own, the difference God makes, Malachi 3:18); 'to morrow shall this sign be' (the sign appointed for the next day, the distinction to be displayed at the set time, proving God's hand, the providence, the distinction a sign that the LORD is in the midst of the earth, v.22, the deliverance of his people marked) — Clarke, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And I will put a division between my people and thy people: to morrow shall this sign be
the division set between Israel and Egypt
the distinction of grace, God's people set apart from the world (Malachi 3:18)
the sign at the appointed time, proving God's hand (v.22)

schools.summary: The plague of flies fulfilled: 'and the LORD did so; and there came a grievous swarm of flies into the house of Pharaoh, and into his servants' houses, and into all the land of Egypt: the land was corrupted by reason of the swarm of flies' — God sending the swarms as threatened (v.21), the grievous (heavy, severe) swarm filling Pharaoh's house, his servants', and all Egypt (the fourth plague fulfilled, Egypt overrun, Goshen spared, v.22, the judgment come); the 'grievous swarm' (the heavy, tormenting plague of insects) — the severe affliction (the loathsome swarms filling all, the judgment heavy on Egypt); 'the land was corrupted by reason of the swarm of flies' (the land ruined, spoiled, by the swarms, the destruction, perhaps the insects devouring and corrupting, the land marred, the heavy judgment, the providence, the plague that would bring Pharaoh's next relenting and compromise, vv.25-28) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke, Ellicott.

themes: And the LORD did so; and there came a grievous swarm of flies into the house of Pharaoh, and into his servants' houses, and into all the land of Egypt: the land was corrupted by reason of the swarm of flies
the plague of flies fulfilled
the grievous swarm filling all Egypt, the land corrupted (v.21)
the fourth plague come, Egypt overrun, Goshen spared (v.22)

schools.summary: Pharaoh's first compromise - sacrifice in the land: 'and Pharaoh called for Moses and for Aaron, and said, Go ye, sacrifice to your God in the land' — Pharaoh, under the plague of flies, calling Moses and Aaron and offering a concession (the relenting under pressure, as with the frogs, v.8, the proud king again forced to deal with the deliverers, the compromise offered); the first compromise — 'Go ye, sacrifice to your God in the land': Pharaoh offering a partial concession — let them sacrifice, but in the land of Egypt (not going the three days' journey into the wilderness, Exodus 5:3; 8:27, the half-yielding, the first of Pharaoh's compromises, by which he sought to keep the people, or hold them in his power, the witnesses noting the lesson — the world's, and Satan's, compromises with God's people: yield something, but not all, serve God, but stay in Egypt, keep your religion, but do not separate from the world, the partial obedience that is no obedience, God requiring the full release, the people wholly set apart, the snare of the half-concession, the attempt to keep God's people in bondage under the guise of allowing their worship); Moses would refuse this compromise (v.26-27) — God requiring the full obedience (the journey out of Egypt, the complete separation, the lesson against half-obedience, the world's offer to let us serve God if we will not separate from it) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

debates.question: What is the snare in Pharaoh's 'sacrifice to your God in the land'?

sides.position: The half-yielding (sacrifice, but stay in Egypt) - the first of Pharaoh's (and the world's) compromises with God's people: serve God, but do not separate from the world; the partial obedience that is no obedience, God requiring the full release and complete separation (Exodus 8:27)

themes: And Pharaoh called for Moses and for Aaron, and said, Go ye, sacrifice to your God in the land
Pharaoh's first compromise - sacrifice in the land (the crux)
the half-yielding - serve God, but stay in Egypt, do not separate from the world
the partial obedience that is no obedience; God requires the full separation (Exodus 8:27), the world's compromise to be refused

schools.summary: Moses refusing the compromise: 'and Moses said, It is not meet so to do; for we shall sacrifice the abomination of the Egyptians to the LORD our God: lo, shall we sacrifice the abomination of the Egyptians before their eyes, and will they not stone us?' — Moses refusing Pharaoh's compromise (v.25): they could not sacrifice in the land, for they would sacrifice 'the abomination of the Egyptians' (animals the Egyptians held sacred, or abhorred to see slain, e.g., the ox or sheep, which some Egyptians worshipped or would not have killed, to do so before the Egyptians would provoke them, the refusal grounded on the offence); the Israelites' sacrifices (of animals the Egyptians held sacred, or the manner of them) would be 'abomination' (offensive, detestable) to the Egyptians — provoking their rage (the reason for going out of the land, to worship without offence or danger); 'shall we sacrifice the abomination of the Egyptians before their eyes, and will they not stone us?' (the danger, the Egyptians, enraged at the slaughter of their sacred animals, would stone the Israelites, the necessity of going out of Egypt, to worship freely and safely, the refusal of the compromise, the full separation required) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Moses said, It is not meet so to do; for we shall sacrifice the abomination of the Egyptians to the LORD our God... will they not stone us?
Moses refusing the compromise
the sacrifices would be abomination to the Egyptians, provoking their rage
the worship requiring the full separation, the half-concession refused (v.25)

schools.summary: Moses insisting on the three days' journey: 'we will go three days' journey into the wilderness, and sacrifice to the LORD our God, as he shall command us' — Moses insisting on the full demand: a three days' journey into the wilderness to sacrifice (Exodus 5:3; 3:18), as God commanded (the refusal of the compromise, no sacrificing in the land, but the full going out, the obedience to God's command, the worship as God appointed, not as Pharaoh allowed); the three days' journey (the going out of Egypt, the separation from the land of bondage) — the full demand (the worship away from Egypt, the separation required); 'as he shall command us' (the worship according to God's command, not man's compromise, the full obedience, the people serving God as he appoints, the refusal of the half-concession, God's command the rule) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: We will go three days' journey into the wilderness, and sacrifice to the LORD our God, as he shall command us
Moses insisting on the three days' journey
the full demand - the going out of Egypt, the separation from bondage (Exodus 5:3; 3:18)
the worship as God commands, not man's compromise

schools.summary: Pharaoh's second compromise - not go very far: 'and Pharaoh said, I will let you go, that ye may sacrifice to the LORD your God in the wilderness; only ye shall not go very far away: intreat for me' — Pharaoh, pressed further, now conceding that they might go into the wilderness to sacrifice (the second concession, a step beyond the first, v.25, the relenting under the plague, the seeming yielding); the snare — 'only ye shall not go very far away': Pharaoh's second compromise — go, but not far (the half-yielding, granting the journey, but limiting it, keeping the people within reach, that he might recall them, the witnesses noting the lesson — another of the world's, and Satan's, compromises: if you must serve God, do not go far from Egypt, do not separate decisively from the world, keep within reach of the bondage, the snare of the half-separation, the attempt to keep God's people near enough to be reclaimed, the partial obedience that God's people must refuse, the full separation required, not a worship that stays within the world's reach); 'intreat for me' (Pharaoh again begging Moses to pray for the removal of the plague, as with the frogs, v.8, the appeal of necessity, not repentance, the relenting that would vanish when the plague was removed, v.32) — JFB, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

debates.question: What is the snare in Pharaoh's 'ye shall not go very far away'?

sides.position: The half-yielding (go, but not far) - another of the world's compromises: serve God, but do not separate decisively from the world; the snare of the half-separation (keeping God's people within reach of the bondage), the partial obedience God's people must refuse, the full separation required

themes: And Pharaoh said, I will let you go, that ye may sacrifice to the LORD your God in the wilderness; only ye shall not go very far away: intreat for me
Pharaoh's second compromise - not go very far (the crux)
the half-yielding - serve God, but do not separate decisively from the world
the snare of the half-separation, keeping God's people within reach; the full separation required

schools.summary: Moses' promise to entreat and his warning against deceit: 'and Moses said, Behold, I go out from thee, and I will intreat the LORD that the swarms of flies may depart from Pharaoh, from his servants, and from his people, to morrow: but let not Pharaoh deal deceitfully any more in not letting the people go to sacrifice to the LORD' — Moses promising to entreat the LORD to remove the flies on the morrow (the appointed time, as the frogs, v.10, the intercession, the removal at the set time, proving God's hand); Moses' warning — 'let not Pharaoh deal deceitfully any more in not letting the people go': Moses warning Pharaoh not to deal deceitfully (as he had with the frogs, breaking his promise when the plague was removed, v.15, the caution against the false relenting, the rebuke of the king's previous treachery); Moses' warning against deceit (Pharaoh's habit of promising under the plague and breaking it after, vv.8, 15, 28) — the rebuke of the false repentance (the providence, Pharaoh would deal deceitfully again, v.32, as Moses foresaw, the warning to the treacherous king) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott.

themes: And Moses said... I will intreat the LORD that the swarms of flies may depart... to morrow: but let not Pharaoh deal deceitfully any more in not letting the people go
Moses' promise to entreat and his warning against deceit
the removal promised at the appointed time (v.10)
the warning against Pharaoh's habitual treachery (vv.8, 15), foreseen (v.32)

schools.summary: Moses interceding for the removal of the flies: 'and Moses went out from Pharaoh, and intreated the LORD' — Moses going out and praying (entreating) the LORD to remove the swarms of flies (as promised, v.29, the intercession, the earnest prayer of the deliverer, the dependence on God for the removal); Moses leaving Pharaoh to pray — the going out to entreat the LORD (the intercessor's resort to God); 'intreated the LORD' (Moses praying for the removal, as with the frogs, v.12, the intercessor seeking the deliverance, the providence, God answering, the flies removed, v.31) — Gill.

themes: And Moses went out from Pharaoh, and intreated the LORD
Moses interceding for the removal of the flies
the earnest prayer of the intercessor (vv.12, 29)
the dependence on God for the removal

schools.summary: The LORD removing the flies completely: 'and the LORD did according to the word of Moses; and he removed the swarms of flies from Pharaoh, from his servants, and from his people; there remained not one' — God answering Moses' prayer (v.30) and removing the flies completely from Pharaoh, his servants, and people (the deliverance fulfilled, the removal at the appointed time, v.29, the complete removal, 'there remained not one' — proving God's hand, the precise, total removal); God answering Moses (the removal at the set time) — the proof of God's hand (the answer to the intercessor); 'there remained not one' (the complete removal, not one fly left, the total deliverance, proving the LORD's hand, the precise removal at the appointed time, the providence, the mercy that should have softened Pharaoh, yet he would harden, v.32) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And the LORD did according to the word of Moses; and he removed the swarms of flies from Pharaoh, from his servants, and from his people; there remained not one
the LORD removing the flies completely
the complete removal at the appointed time, proving God's hand (v.29)
the mercy that Pharaoh would abuse (v.32)

schools.summary: Pharaoh hardening again after the flies: 'and Pharaoh hardened his heart at this time also, neither would he let the people go' — Pharaoh, the flies removed (v.31), hardening his heart again (as with the frogs, v.15) and refusing to let the people go (the repeated self-hardening, the false relenting vanishing when the plague was lifted, the broken promise, the deceit Moses had warned against, v.29); 'at this time also' (as before, vv.15) — the repeated hardening (the pattern of the false repentance, relenting under the plague, hardening when relieved, the obstinacy growing with the mercies abused, Romans 2:4-5); 'neither would he let the people go' (the broken promise, Pharaoh's deceit, v.29, the hardened refusal, the providence, the obstinacy that would draw down the heavier plagues, ch. 9-11, and prove the more, by God's overruling, the LORD's power, Exodus 9:16; Romans 9:17) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Pharaoh hardened his heart at this time also, neither would he let the people go
Pharaoh hardening again after the flies
the repeated self-hardening, the false relenting vanishing when the plague was lifted (v.15)
the deceit Moses foretold (v.29), the obstinacy drawing the heavier plagues (Romans 2:4-5)
